Just a natural evolution from the 2-3-5 / 3-2-5 shape teams were using a few years ago in attack. Now, with so much emphasis on man-to-man pressing, some teams are pushing that extra player forward to again try to stretch the defence and gain numerical advantages higher up the park, not to mention better counter-pressing opportunities. Teams that do this are more vulnerable to counters but will generally be sides with strong, fast defenders who are comfortable in 1v1 duels. You won't see Scotland do this, but France, Spain, Germany and the likes are sides with profiles that could play this way.

>Teams that do this are more vulnerable to counters but will generally be sides with strong, fast defenders comfortable in 1v1 duels. Well this part of the tactics didn't really work for Germany at the last 2 world cups :D Ivory Coast and Ecuador might have more quality to exploit it than Curacao

The 424/442 was invented specifically to counter Herbert Chapman's 3223, which itself was invented to counter the WW formation that Pep's Barcelona like to use. We can already see the next stage coming with teams utilising the 3142 or 3133 shape (Cryuff) to try and break the 442 to great effect. Sweden's dismantling of Tunisia was the most recent example.

The WM is the 3223 he referenced. The 424 was a brazilian invention to counter the 3223 that was very popular at the time I am not too sure on the timeline regarding the WW though, from what I recall it was used by Hungary back when they were good in the 50s but he is arguing it predates the WM so I am kinda lost too. But yeah WM and WW were two different formations

In England 2323 predates the 3223. This is why the sideback positions are called full backs and are numbered 2 and 3. You have 2 fullbacks, 3 halfbacks, 2 outside forwards/wingers, 2 inside forwards and 1 centreforwards Herbert Chapman dropped one of the 3 halfbacks in between the 2 fullbacks to change 2-3-5 to 3-2-5. Later another half back was dropped to form a back 4 and one of the inside forwards (8) was dropped into halfback position to form the 424.

Out of curiosity, what is the lore on the 424 over there in england? Here in Brasil it is stated that it was created here in the 50s as an answer to the WM, its the formation we used to win 58 and 62. It is said that adding another attacker + forming a more solid back 4 that enabled fullbacks to overlap like crazy not only created some imbalances for the back 3 used by the WM but also made it so that individual man marking (supposedly popular at the time) had a hard time defending. Is this how you guys learn about it in england? Curious to know!

There was a lot of cross pollination of ideas in the 50s.. Brazil winning the world cup made 424 pretty popular. In UK they basically drop 2 halfbacks into the backline and shift the fullbacks wide, and shift one of the inside midfield beside the one remaining halfback. The issue with a 424 is that wingers are not that useful against a back 4 compared to a back 3, plus the midfield 2 always gets overrun... So managers in Europe, especially teams that are less talented, started to drop one of the wingers into midfield to create a 433 with one inside forward and one winger. Alf Ramsey later decided to abandon the wings all together and used the 4312/442 to win the world cup 66 which stuck for quite some time.

Any discussion of an ideal midfield for Brazil that doesn't include Danilo Santos shows that the person really doesn't understand the current form of Brazil's players. Danilo Santos played well every second he was on the field, whether in the friendlies against France, Croatia or Panama. He needs to be a starter, and that’s one of Ancelotti’s biggest mistakes. Brazil's ideal midfield lineup is: Bruno Guimarães, Danilo Santos and Cunha/Paquetá. In that case, yes, the team could defend in a 4-4-2 formation with Cunha covering the left side instead of Vini. I just don't know if he can handle that role. But with Brazil in possession, we need wide wingers (Vini and Rayan/Luiz Henrique). Ancelotti made the wrong choice at left fullback, and on the right, we’ve lost our top three options to injury (Militão, Wesley and Vanderson)... So, all of our fullbacks are defensive. My ideal team would be: * Alisson; * Danilo * Marquinhos * Gabriel Magalhães * Douglas Santos * Bruno Guimarães * Danilo Santos * Rayan * Matheus Cunha or Lucas Paquetá * Vinicius Junior * Endrick A standard 4-2-3-1 formation that switches to a 4-4-2 in defensive phase. **EDIT:** “Where's Raphinha?” Well, Raphinha's last good game for Brazil was the 4-1 victory over Uruguay in 2021. He shouldn't be in the starting lineup based on how he plays for Barcelona. This motherfucker needs to be benched. And I know people will say that Vinicius Junior didn't play well for Brazil either, but Vinicius was one of Brazil's best players in the 2022 World Cup. I'm coming to the conclusion that he rises to the occasion when the stakes are high.

Jude Bellingham has to be the weirdest player profile I've seen in my life. He's neither a pure 8 like Scholes/Lampard/Gerrard despite having better feet than all 3 and he clearly isn't a pure "magic man" #10 either. He's talented enough to do a job in almost any position you play him but he doesn't really have a true position yet where he actually dominates matches.
Jude in theory has some of the traits that made Pogba and Yaya such unique talents, but he doesn’t have the passing range or magic of either of them.
He's a world class version of Elias, but I'm afraid most people here don't know ball like that

He's an 8 but he got the reputation of a goalscoring 10 and can't seem to shake the tag And honestly, against the big sides that'll actually be good for us, because Rice and Anderson behind him makes sense For games like today though he had to either be alongside Rogers advanced or dropping deeper for him and sit next to rice

Thing I'd be slightly scared about (especially in games like this) when it comes to playing him deeper and next to Rice is the Gerrard/Lampard conundrum - basically them being a surprisingly similar type of players with slightly different strengths, neither of whom really is that metronomic passer who's just happy sitting a bit deeper and moving the ball around safely to create openings. They both love a rush forward, they're both physical, they both can put their stamp on games in slightly different areas of the pitch, but their skillsets aren't really additive when playing next to each other. In a game like tonight that *might* help unlock Ghana, but it just as well might lead to being too open on the break and ultimately conceding. And rolling the dice like that in just the second group game doesn't strike me as a sensible decision, especially when the entire side isn't quite on it. Because for me that was the real problem with England tonight: It was all a bit ponderous, it lacked incisiveness, and I'm not sure you'll fix that with Bellingham/Rice at the base of midfield.
